entry.title: Configure Standalone Selling Price Using Pricing Conditions in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud
entry.content:
Introduction 

Under IFRS 15 and ASC 606, every performance obligation in a multi-element arrangement must be allocated a portion of the transaction price based on its Standalone Selling Price (SSP). But how does SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud actually know what the SSP is for a given product or service? 

The answer lies in a set of pre-configured pricing condition types that the system uses to automatically pass the SSP into Universal Revenue Recognition (URR). In this post, we walk through how these condition types work, where to configure them, and how they appear in the revenue contract — illustrated with a concrete example using a solution order. 

In SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud, a Solution Order can contain multiple business objects — each representing a different line of business — and the way the SSP is defined depends on which business object is involved.  

Sales Item / Service Contract 

The SSP is defined via the standard Pricing Procedure of the Solution Order. SSP condition types (PSAM, PSA1, PSA2) are included in that pricing procedure, and the SSP value is maintained via Manage Prices using condition records in the product master. The calculated value flows automatically to URR. 

Subscription Billing 

Subscription Billing does not use a pricing procedure. Instead, the SSP condition type is defined as an output condition in the Pricing Element Specification. The total amount of PSAM/PSA1/PSA2 is then passed to URR as the SSP of the performance obligation (POB). The detailed configuration for Subscription Billing is covered in a separate blog post — see [link to be added]. 

Customer Project 

For professional services delivered via a Customer Project, there is no pricing procedure and no condition record in the product master. The SSP is entered manually directly in the project when assigning resources. That manually entered value is then converted into the PSAS condition type in the relevant sales order, from where it flows to URR as the SSP of the POB. 

Regardless of which business object is used, URR always receives the total amount of PSAM or PSAS as the SSP value of the performance obligation. The source mechanism differs, but the outcome is consistent. 

The Four Pre-Configured SSP Condition Types 

SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud ships with four standard pricing condition types specifically designated as SSP carriers. You can find them in the configuration activity: 

Manage Pricing → Set Condition Types for Pricing in Sales  

Condition Type 

Description 

Typical Use Case 

PSAM 

Quantity-based SSP 

Standard sales orders, product-based items 

PSAS 

Manually entered SSP 

Professional services (no product master price) 

PSA1 

Monthly-based SSP 

Service contracts — monthly recurring charges 

PSA2 

Daily-based SSP 

Service contracts — daily recurring charges 

Each of these condition types signals to URR that this value represents the standalone selling price of the performance obligation. 

PSAM, PSI1, and PSI2 are automatically included in the standard pricing procedure for solution orders (e.g. A10024) since SAP Public Cloud 2608. They are added at the end of the pricing procedure and — crucially — are marked as Statistical. This means they do not affect the net value of the document; they exist solely to communicate the SSP to revenue accounting. 

Requirement = 2 means, that only when the product has the pricing information maintained, then the pricing information will be displayed. 

Note: If you copy the standard pricing procedure to create your own, make sure you also mark these condition types as Statistical in your custom procedure. 

Note: If you use subscription billing, you can use any of the condition types above.  

Where to Maintain the SSP Value 

Product-Based conditions (PSAM / PSI1 / PSI2) 

For products sold via sales orders or service contracts, the SSP is maintained in the product master: 

Product Master → Pricing Management → Set Material Price for Sales 

Here you define the SSP per condition type. For example: 

  • PSAM = €20.80 per piece (quantity-based, for a standard product) 

  • PSI1 = €50 per month (monthly-based, for a service contract item) 

 

Professional Services (PSAS) 

For professional services, there is no access sequence — meaning the SSP is not derived from the product master. Instead, it is entered manually when you assign resources to the project and capture the demand. The person entering the resource assignment inputs the SSP directly (e.g. €6,000 for a consulting engagement). That value is then converted into the PSAS condition type in the relevant sales order. 

 

A Walk-Through Example: Solution Order 

Let's bring this to life with a solution order containing three business objects: a sales order item, a service contract, and a customer project for professional services. 

Step 1 — Create the Solution Order 

A solution order is created with: 

  • A sales order item (e.g. Operio) with quantity 1 
  • A service contract item with 1 unit over 12 months 
  • A project-based professional services item (Customer Project) 

 

Step 2 — Review Pricing Details 

Opening the pricing details of the solution order, you can see: 

  • For the sales order item: PSAM is picked up from the product master at €20.80 per piece. With a quantity of 5, the SSP value passed to URR is €104.00 (20.80 × 5) 

 

  • For the service contract: PSI1 is picked up at €50 per month. Over 12 months, the SSP value is €600 (50 × 12). PSAM does not appear here because Requirement 2 ensures only the relevant condition type is shown per item type. 

  • For the customer project: PSAS is entered manually as €5,000 when the resource is assigned. It is then converted into the PSAS condition type in the sales order. 

Step 3 — Revenue Contract 

Once the solution order is released, a revenue contract is created automatically. As you can see below the SSP is based on the condition type value in the app ‘Monitor Revenue Contract’ 

  

In the Manage Revenue Accounting Items app, you will see the SSP values transferred per condition  

Business Object 

Condition Type 

SSP Value 

Sales order item 

PSAM 

€10,400 

Service contract 

PSI1 

€600 

Customer project (PS) 

PSAS 

€6,000 

 

Revenue accounting always receives the complete SSP value per item, regardless of how that value was computed (quantity-based, time-based, or manual). The allocation of the transaction price across performance obligations is then performed based on these relative SSP values. 

Key Takeaways 

  • Four condition types (PSAM, PSAS, PSI1, PSI2) are pre-configured in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud to carry the SSP into URR. 
  • These condition types must be marked as Statistical in the pricing procedure — they do not affect the document net value. 
  • SSP values are maintained in the product master for sales order and service contract items, and entered manually for professional services. 
  • SSP is passed through the URR with the condition types above carrying the amount.

entry.title: RF Device EWM SAP - S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition
entry.content:
Hi SAP Team,

We are implementing embedded EWM in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud and need to enable RF Device/RF Framework functionality for warehouse operations.

Kindly clarify the following:

  1. Is activation of scope item 63V (RF Framework for S/4HANA Cloud Supply Chain) PERSON_NAME to use RF Device transactions in Public Cloud EWM?
  2. Is there any additional licensing requirement/cost associated with activating this scope item?
  3. Are there any prerequisites or dependent scope items required before activation?
  4. Any known restrictions or limitations of RF Framework in Public Cloud compared to Private Cloud/On-Premise.

Request you to confirm at the earliest as this is blocking our RF configuration for the ongoing implementation.

 


------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Learn more about the SAP Support user and program here.

entry.title: Re: RF Device EWM SAP - S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition
entry.content:
Dear SAP Community,

Regarding your questions about using RF devices in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition:

1. Is scope item 63V mandatory to use RF devices?

Yes. If you intend to use the standard RF (Radio Frequency) framework in Embedded EWM, the corresponding scope item 63V – Mobile RF Devices in Warehousing must be activated in your implementation project. This scope item delivers the standard RF functionality for warehouse operations in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition.

2. Is there any additional licensing required?

No. There is no separate license required specifically for scope item 63V. The RF functionality is part of the standard Embedded EWM capabilities available in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition.

3. Are there any prerequisites?

The following prerequisites should be fulfilled:

  • Embedded EWM must be implemented.
  • Scope item 63V must be activated in SAP Central Business Configuration (CBC).
  • Warehouse configuration (warehouse number, storage types, bins, etc.) must be completed.
  • Appropriate business roles and RF user settings must be maintained.
  • Presentation devices and RF user settings should be configured using the corresponding Fiori apps.

4. Are there any restrictions in Public Cloud?

The RF framework in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition uses SAP GUI for HTML for productive execution. Unlike Private Cloud or On-Premise, Public Edition does not support customer modifications of the RF framework or custom RF menu development. Only standard RF functionality and limited UI adaptations (for example, via Screen Personas/Adapt UI) are supported. Additionally, there is no need to configure ITS Mobile or communication arrangements, as RF runs natively through SAP GUI for HTML.

Helpful SAP references

The following SAP Notes/KBAs provide additional information:

  • KBA 3671646 - FAQ: Usage of RF (Radio Frequency) Devices in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition - SAP for Me  – FAQ: Usage of RF (Radio Frequency) Devices in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition (overview of RF functionality, supported devices, productive usage, Test RF Environment, restrictions, and best practices).
  • KBA 3672406 - Resource Management for RF device in Fiori Apps for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition - SAP for Me  – Resource Management for RF Device in Fiori Apps for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition (configuration of presentation devices and user settings).
  • SAP Note 3048632 - Information and restrictions for mobile data terminals in Warehouse Management in S/4HANA Cloud or EWM in SAP S/4HANA On-Premise - SAP for Me – Information and restrictions for mobile data terminals in Warehouse Management in SAP S/4HANA Cloud (referenced by KBA 3671646).
  • SAP Note 3340441 - Recommendation for presentation devices and browsers when using RF with SAP GUI for HTML - SAP for Me  – Recommendation for presentation devices and browsers when using RF with SAP GUI for HTML (referenced by KBA 3671646).
  • SAP Note 3364010 - FAQ: EWM RF transactions using SAP GUI for HTML - SAP for Me  – FAQ: EWM RF transactions using SAP GUI for HTML (referenced by KBA 3671646).

Relevant SAP Community discussions

The following SAP Community Q&A may also be helpful:

  • Do we need to use BTP with scope item 63V in order to use RF devices?
    This confirms that SAP BTP is not required for the standard RF functionality in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition and recommends following the setup guide for scope item 63V.
  • How to use RF scanner in the EWM S/4HANA Cloud system?
    This explains how productive RF devices access the RF framework using SAP GUI for HTML, provides the productive URL pattern, and recommends the relevant SAP Notes and KBAs for device compatibility and setup.

Hope this helps, please let us know if you have any other concern!

Best regards,

SAP Support

entry.title: Re: Questions regarding BC Upgrade procedures (Manual Tra...
entry.content:
Dear @Harukiy,

Question 1

No, the What’s New Viewer and RASD alone are not sufficient and your practice of reviewing the PDF attachments to SAP Note 3646210 is appropriate. RASD uses the same Preliminary What’s New and final What’s New information as the What’s New Viewer, filtered according to the activated and used scope in your system. These sources cover documented new, changed and deleted functionality but they are not an exhaustive list of every application correction delivered in an individual Hotfix Collection. The HFC PDFs are therefore an important complementary source. Since these PDFs provide only short descriptions, targeted regression testing of critical business processes after the relevant maintenance window remains the final validation step

Question 2

HFC12 was released together with feature update 2602.4 on June 11, 2026. The standard software update is deployed by SAP according to the maintenance schedule. Your manual transport settings control the transport of Business Configuration and extensibility content between your systems they do not control SAP deployment of the HFC itself. The standard screen could therefore change independently of a manual BC transport

I checked both the official final What’s New document for 2602.4 and the HFC12 Hotfix Short Description PDF but I could not find an explicit entry describing this particular ATP Check Selection Screen change. Since RASD uses the same What’s New content, I would not expect a separate RASD entry for this specific change. Product Assistance may reflect the current application behavior but it does not by itself establish when a particular UI adjustment was delivered

Based on the published documentation the exact delivery responsible for this change cannot be identified. If confirmation is required, I would open a case with SAP Support and provide the application name or ID the before and after screenshots the system version and the date when the change became visible

More details below:

SAP KBA 1296527 - How to create a support case for SAP Support - SAP for Me

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: Re: Tengo 3 consultas que realizar sobre modulo de Manufa...
entry.content:
Dear @Nestor_Ramirez,

Question 1.

New production order types can be created in SAP CBC using the Define Production Order Types configuration activity. SAP documents the recommended approach as copying an existing production order type and adjusting it to the required process

The order category remains fixed as category 10 Production Order. Plant-specific settings must additionally be maintained in Define Order-Type-Dependent Parameters for Production Orders, where parameters related to master-data selection, planning, execution and cost accounting are defined. Configuration is therefore possible according to the business requirement but only within the configuration options released by SAP for Public Cloud

Qeustion 2.

New standard value keys can be created using the Define Standard Value Key configuration activity

 

A standard value key can contain up to six standard values, for example setup, machine and labor time. The required parameters are maintained in Define Parameters and can then be used in formulas for scheduling, capacity requirements and costing. The new standard value key must subsequently be assigned to the relevant work centers or resources

Question 3.

A production order without a header material can be created using the Create Production Order Without Material (CO07) app

A WBS element can be used as a non-material settlement receiver in the supported Event-Based Production Cost Posting scenario. However a production order without material referenced to a WBS element is not supported in the Engineer-to-Order scenario. If the requirement is production for project stock the Create Production Order with Project (CO10) app must be used. This scenario requires both a material and a WBS element belonging to a project with project stock

More details below:

SAP Learning – Configuring Production Order Types

SAP Learning – Configuring Manufacturing Master Data

SAP Help – Create Production Order Without Material (CO07)

SAP Help – Settlement Rules of Manufacturing Orders

SAP Community – Production Order Settlement to WBS Elements

SAP Help – Create Production Order with Project (CO10)

SAP KBA 3720746 - Error CO831 in 'Production Order Without Material - Create' (CO07) app - SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition

SAP KBA 3691249 - None-material ETO order is not supported - SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: Re: Baseline date and net due date mismatch in the Import...
entry.content:
Hi Kazuhiro,

Thanks — that clarifies the target state, and it's straightforward to configure. It's the same record we were discussing: whichever day-limit entry under Z120 is selected by the day of your baseline date (the 10th) is the one you need to change.

What to change

In Maintain Terms of Payment (SSCUI 102934), open that record and:

  • Clear all three No. of Days lines (including the "1" in term line 1) and leave the percentages at 0. With no period maintained, the item is due on the baseline date — that alone removes the +1 day you saw.
  • Make sure Fixed Day and Additional Months are empty on that record, and Default for baseline date = Posting Date. Otherwise the baseline itself moves, and the due date follows it. Reference: KBA 2926267

That gives you exactly Net payment days = 0 and Baseline date = Net due date.

One constraint to plan around

The Day Limit of an existing payment term cannot be edited in Public Cloud — only the values inside each record. So if Z120 has several day-limit records, you have to repeat the change on every one of them, otherwise invoices dated in another range will still behave differently. If the day-limit split has no business purpose anymore, it is cleaner to create a new key (e.g. Z121) with a single record, Day Limit blank, baseline default = Posting Date, no term lines, and reassign it in the supplier's company-code data. Reference: KBA 2926267

Holidays stay as they are

As mentioned before, the non-workday correction is a separate mechanism driven by the calendar, not by the number of days — setting the periods to 0 does not disable it, so the bank-holiday shift you already use continues to apply on top of the new due date. Reference: Shifting Payment Due Dates That Fall on Bank Holidays

Verify

Post one test invoice with a posting date in the changed day-limit range and check the Payment tab: Days 1 = 0 and Net Due Date = Baseline Date. Then repeat via F3041. Already posted documents are not recalculated, so this applies to new invoices only.

BR, Michal

entry.title: SAP Inside Track Munich 2026 | Call for Speakers 📢 #sitMUC26
entry.content:
Dear SAP Community,

On October 10th, the SAP community will gather for the 10th time (yey!) once again in Garching for a day full of learning, sharing, and connecting... and we want you to be part of it.

Whether you're deep into ABAP, building apps on SAP BTP, working with Fiori, S/4HANA, or Analytics, exploring DevOps in SAP, or shaping business processes, driving change in projects, or navigating transformation from a consulting perspective, your insights matter. Our audience comes from all corners of the SAP universe: developers, consultants, architects, product experts, and curious newcomers. That’s why we’re looking for diverse session ideas: technical deep dives, lessons learned from projects, open-source initiatives, or even side projects that others can learn from. It doesn’t have to be perfect or polished. If it’s real and relevant to the community, it belongs on stage.

Important dates and submission process:

  • Call opens: July 19st
  • Submission deadline: August 30th
  • Speaker selection: by September 13th
  • Please submit your proposal via email: speakers@sitmuc.com

There are various session types for your submissions:

  • Short Presentation (20 min)
  • Presentation (40 min)
    you will present a topic to the audience demos, theory, live coding, or anything else that suits your expertise
  • Marketplace
    you will share your topic in a “marketplace” breakout session where everyone is welcome to walk around, visit the various stands, and interact

What we’re looking for:

  • On-site only we’ll be together in person!
  • No marketing pitches – just genuine value for the community

We’d love to hear your ideas whether you’re a seasoned speaker or presenting for the very first time. Let’s make #sitMUC26 another unforgettable community event.

If you're unsure about your topic, format, or how to present it no worries! Just drop us an email at speakers@sitmuc.com, we're happy to help. 

Let’s build this day together. Bring your voice, your story, your topic. We can’t wait to hear it!

Best regards,
sitMUC Orga team

entry.title: Re: 🐍 July Developer Challenge Week 2 — Roll & Move
entry.content:
Start-Problems

The type: module made a problem.
To keep this setting, the Exports-Statement in game-service.js had to be changed.

module.exports = class GameService extends cds.ApplicationService { ..

export default class GameService extends cds.ApplicationService { ...

 

Tasks


1. Your srv/game-service.js handler implementations (paste as a JS code block)

 

import cds from '@sap/cds'

export default class GameService extends cds.ApplicationService {
  async init() {

    const { Players, BoardSquares } = this.entities

    // ── startTurn ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    // @from: [#Waiting]  →  CAP validates status before this runs
    // @TO: #Playing      →  CAP updates status after this returns
    // No business logic needed — CAP's @flow.status handles the transition.
    this.on('startTurn', 'Players', async (req) => {
      // Intentionally empty: the @from/@to annotations do all the work.
    })

    // ── rollDice ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    // @from: [#Playing]  →  CAP validates status before this runs
    // @TO: #Moving       →  CAP updates status after this returns
    this.on('rollDice', 'Players', async (req) => {
      const player = await SELECT.one.from(Players).where({ ID: req.params[0].ID })
      if (!player) return req.error(404, 'Player not found')

      const roll = Math.ceil(Math.random() * 6)
      let newPosition = player.position + roll

      // M.L. 19.07.2026 war nur zum Test, um Würfeln zu umgehen. 
      //let newPosition = player.position + 1

      // Can't overshoot square 100 — must land exactly
      if (newPosition > 100) {
        await UPDATE(Players).set({ lastRoll: roll }).where({ ID: player.ID })
        const needed = 100 - player.position
        return req.reject(409, `Need exactly ${needed} to finish. Rolled ${roll}. No move.`)
      }

      // Resolve snake or ladder on the new square
      const square = await SELECT.one.from(BoardSquares).where({ square: newPosition })
      let finalPosition = newPosition
      let event = null

      if (square?.ladderTo) {
        finalPosition = square.ladderTo
        event = { type: 'ladder', 'from': newPosition, 'to': finalPosition }
      } else if (square?.snakeTo) {
        finalPosition = square.snakeTo
        event = square.isDoubleHead
          ? { type: 'doubleSnake', 'from': newPosition, 'to': finalPosition }
          : { type: 'snake', 'from': newPosition, 'to': finalPosition }
      }

      // Persist position and last roll
      await UPDATE(Players)
        .set({ position: finalPosition, lastRoll: roll })
        .where({ ID: player.ID })

      // Emit domain event so subscribers (Week 3) can react
      if (event) await this.emit('BoardEvent', { playerID: player.ID, ...event })

      // Use req.reply() — not return — to pin the response value before CAP's
      // @flow.status after-handler runs. A plain `return` gets cleared by the
      // flow machinery, producing HTTP 204 instead of the expected 200 + body.
      req.reply({ roll, position: finalPosition, event: event?.type ?? 'normal' })
    })

    // ── confirmMove ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    // @from: [#Moving]  →  normal end of a turn, hands off to next player (Week 3)
    // @TO: #Waiting     →  player waits for their next turn
    this.on('confirmMove', 'Players', async (req) => {
      // Week 3 will add turn rotation here.
      // For now: just let CAP apply @TO: #Waiting
    })

    // ── blockPlayer ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    // @from: [#Moving]  →  called when player lands on a double-headed snake
    // @TO: #Blocked     →  player skips their next turn
    this.on('blockPlayer', 'Players', async (req) => {
      // Business logic: the position was already updated in rollDice.
      // This action just transitions state — CAP handles the @TO: #Blocked update.
      req.info('Player is blocked and will skip their next turn.')
    })

    // ── unblockPlayer ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    // @from: [#Blocked]   →  called at the start of a blocked player's next turn
    // @TO: $flow.previous →  CAP restores the status to whatever it was before #Blocked
    this.on('unblockPlayer', 'Players', async (req) => {
      // CAP's $flow.previous does the work — no code needed here.
      // The player's status returns to #Waiting automatically.
    })

    // ── winGame ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    // @from: [#Moving]  →  called when player.position === 100
    // @TO: #Finished    →  player has won
    this.on('winGame', 'Players', async (req) => {
      const player = await SELECT.one.from(Players).where({ ID: req.params[0].ID })
      if (!player) return req.error(404, 'Player not found')


      // M.L. 19.07.2026 Idea from waseem1713 
      if (player.position !== 100) {
        return req.reject(400, `Player must be on square 100 to win. Current position: ${player.position}`)
      } 

      // Mark the session as finished with this player as winner
      await UPDATE('snakeladder.GameSessions')
        .set({
          finishedAt: new Date().toISOString(),
          winner_ID: player.ID
        })
        .where({ ID: player.session_ID })

      await this.emit('GameWon', { playerID: player.ID, sessionID: player.session_ID })
    })


    this.on('skipTurn', 'Players', async (req) => {
      const player = await SELECT.one.from(Players).where({ ID: req.params[0].ID })
      await UPDATE(Players)
        .set({ turnsBlocked: (player.turnsBlocked || 0) + 1 })
        .where({ ID: player.ID })
    })




    return super.init()
  }
}

2. A screenshot showing the HTTP 409 response when calling rollDice in the wrong state

 



3. A screenshot or OData response showing your player's position after landing on a snake or ladder



4. One sentence: what happens if you call winGame on a player whose position is not 100? How does your implementation handle it?

The Turn-Status is set to “Finished“, without any check.

 

To Intercept that i added a checker (like waseem1713)

if (player.position !== 100) {

        return req.reject(400, `Player must be on square 100 to win. Current position: ${player.position}`)

      }



Stretch-Goal (Skip Turn)

Description:

The Flow-Status is set to previous via annotation.
Additional a counter is set via event-handling on Skip-Turn

Process:

Blocking

 

Skip Turn

entry.title: Hotfix Collection Application Changes after the 2608 Upgrade of the SAP ERP Cloud Public Edition
entry.content:
Introduction

In my recent blog Getting Ready for the 2608 Upgrade of the SAP Cloud ERP Public Edition, I discussed the major changes in upgrade procedure, and the Hotfix Collection (HFC) application pattern change. However, when I discussed the topic with my colleagues, I noticed it is still confusing to understand how the HFC application is done. Therefore, I am going to explain the topic further in this short blog.

Background and Motivation

One loud feedback we heard from our customers is that too many weekend maintenance work in the SAP ERP Cloud Public Edition. We aim to reduce this disruption to customer’s weekend work and harmonize the cloud maintenance operations at the same weekend for multiple SAP cloud products. This becomes the motivation of introducing the new HFC application process.

New HFC Application Process from September 2026

In the SAP Document SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, 3-System Landscape Upgrade & Maintenance Schedule, it has one slide titled “Maintenance Calendar”.  I screenshot a portion of that busy table for our discussion as seen below.

HFC Application Schedule after 2608 Upgrade

 Let me focus on the Column October in this blog, but you can apply our discussion to other months. We assume four weekends per month.  Every weekend, we have four hours Contractual Maintenance Periods (CMP).  This is still in place as part of our contract.  Until now, we used most of it. However, from now on, we will try to use as less as possible to reduce the disruption.

Standard Contractual Maintenance Periods (CMP)

First, we pretty much moved the old infrastructure related maintenance to be online. Therefore, there is no more dark-blue weekend.  Then, we combined all the work needs downtime into one called the Standard Maintenance Weekend, shown as dark-green like October 10-11.  Subsequently, we have one more row of light-green on Oct. 17-18, called the Online Software Change Weekend. Of course, we still have the Optional Maintenance Weekend (not shown) on a needed basis, as part of the Contractual Maintenance Periods.  All of these weekend work are system maintenance related.

Next comes the HFC itself. Before moving on, I talk about a new technique we use to apply HFC: When applying HFC to the P tenant, the Blue Green Deployment Method (see my blog) is used. This creates a “near zero downtime maintenance” (nZDM).  The downtime is less than 1 min on average to switch the database schema from the old content version to the new content version. To users, this is a no downtime applying a HFC.  In contrast, for non-P tenants, we still use the conventional method and it takes 30 to 60 min downtime to apply a HFC.

In addition, we changed the biweekly HFC release schedule to once a month, such as HFC05 for October, HFC06 for November, etc. One week later, we release a critical bug fix HFCxx+, no new features are added. By looking at the October column closely, we can observe:

  1. The HFC05 is only applied to the T and other tenants. This creates a one-week Preview window to check out the new features in the T tenant.
  2. For the P and D tenants, we only apply HFC05+, aka the bug free version of HFC05 to it.  This makes P and D tenants more stable.
  3. For the P tenant, it gets HFC05+ without a downtime.
  4. Only two weekends are impacted by HFC application. Another two weekends are free of maintenance, unless there is an urgent repair needs to be carried out.

When we focus on the entire month of P tenant, we notice that it only receives one downtime (dark-green) weekend out of four!

In summary, we can see the benefits of the new HFC application process as following:

  • The HFC is released monthly, and the D/P Tenants receive a bug free HFC
  • Creating a one-week preview window on T tenant for each HFC content, before the D/P Tenants receive the same.  This is consistent with the major upgrade (two-week preview window).
  • The downtime disruption to customer’s P tenant is drastically reduced (4 vs. 1 in a month):
    • Only one downtime weekend each month for Standard Maintenance.
    • The HFCxx+ is applied without a downtime.
    • Two weekends are free of any maintenance activities.
  • The downtime Standard Maintenance Window is harmonized with other SAP Cloud Products to synchronize the maintenance schedule.

 

Summary

By listening to our customers, we took the advantage of new maintenance techniques and regrouped maintenance activities, the impact of applying HFC to customers are drastically reduced after the 2608 Upgrade.

 

References

  • My blog: Understanding Upgrading and Patching Processes of SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition
  • My blog: Getting Ready for the 2608 Upgrade of the SAP Cloud ERP Public Edition
  • SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, 3-System Landscape Upgrade & Maintenance Schedule (February 19, 20...
  • SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition Software Lifecycle Management for Customers (September 3, 2025)

entry.title: Unexpected Ariba Installation Provisioned on Account – Request for Investigation and Decommissioning
entry.content:
An SAP Ariba installation has been provisioned under our SAP for Me account, although we did not request this service and do not believe we have any Ariba entitlements associated with our contracts. Could SAP Cloud Operations please investigate why this Ariba installation was provisioned on our account? we request that the installation be decommissioned.


--- Steps to Reproduce ---

1. Log in to SAP for Me.2. Navigate to Services & Support > Systems & Provisioning > Systems.3. Search for Installation DIGITS.4. Open the installation details.5. Observe that the installation exists under our account.6. Observe that the "Created By" field is blank and no requester or entitlement details are visible



--- Issue Date (UTC) ---
DIGITS-07-11 20:00:00

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Learn more about the SAP Support user and program here.

entry.title: Re: Unexpected Ariba Installation Provisioned on Account...
entry.content:
I understand you have some questions regarding a new Ariba installation which was created under your customer number 1685040.

There are a couple different ways installation numbers get created which I will explain. I will also provide information on who to contact if you need further details on who/why an installation was created by SAP.

 

To view 'who' created your company's various installations, go to https://me.sap.com/app/installations and find the "Created By" column. 

On-premise installation numbers are usually requested by your company's S-users in SAP for Me using the self-service option according to KBA 0001773826. Creating this way will show the S-user ID in the "Created By" column.

Cloud installation numbers usually start with 90* and are created by "SAP". 

If the "Created By" doesn't list a S-user ID and shows Created By "SAP", you can assume the installation was created by SAP through some internal process or when a new contract (new product) is added to the account -- this includes On Premise as well as Cloud.

This issue is documented in KBA 3564759 - Notification for a New Installation Number - SAP for Me 

 

When a new installation is created (whether it is an on-premise installation requested by an S-user or a cloud installation automatically generated from your contract), an email notification is automatically sent to all S-users who hold the authorization object "Manage Installations."

If you need clarification regarding who created the installation or why it was created, please contact SAP Order Management using SAP KBA 1660069, or reach out directly to your responsible SAP Account Manager as below:

They are responsible for contractual matters and can provide details about the purchased product and why the installation number has been created.
